Fixed. The mock netcfg-setup package was missing pname, so parseDrvName "mock-netcfg-setup" returned "mock-netcfg-setup"
 instead of "netcfg-setup", which didn't match the bootEssentialNames list. This caused it to be placed in the managed profile
 (/nix/system/profile/bin/) instead of flat-copied to usr/bin/, failing the artifact test.

 The cargo test failure (cannot find -lc) is the documented pre-existing cross-compilation limitation — not caused by any of
 these changes.

 ### Infrastructure created

 - mk-c-library.nix — Cross-compilation builder with:
     - mkLibrary — base builder with full cross-env setup
     - mkAutotools — ./configure && make && make install
     - mkCmake — CMake with cross-compilation toolchain
     - Uses clang + relibc sysroot, produces static .a +
 headers

 ### What this unlocks next

 With zlib + openssl + expat available as buildInputs, we
 can now cross-compile:
 - curl (needs zlib + openssl) → HTTP client
 - git (needs zlib + openssl + expat) → version control
 - libpng (needs zlib) → image support → freetype2 →
 fontconfig → graphics stack
 - ncurses + readline → bash, vim, htop, nano
 - Any Rust crate with -sys dependencies on these (e.g.,
 openssl-sys, zlib-sys)

 ### What We Did

 1. Ran the self-hosting test (nix run .#self-hosting-test) — first time ever
 2. Diagnosed 3 layered issues preventing rustc -vV from working on Redox:

 ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────┐
 │ Issue                                                 │ Fix                                                       │ Status    │
 ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┤
 │ randd rejects reads from SchemeRoot handles           │ Patched read() to accept SchemeRoot                       │ ✅ Fixed  │
 ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┤
 │ librustc_driver.so needs libstdc++.so.6 (missing on   │ Built libstdcxx-shim — shared libc++ as libstdc++.so.6    │ ✅ Fixed  │
 │ Redox)                                                │ (943 symbols)                                             │           │
 ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┤
 │ ld_so corrupts scheme fd state during dynamic library │ Root cause found, needs relibc/kernel fix                 │ ❌        │
 │ loading                                               │                                                           │ Blocker   │
 └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────┘

 ### Current Test Results: 13/17 pass

 - ✅ All toolchain presence (rustc, cargo, cc, lld, clang, sysroot, libc.a, headers, CRT, librustc_driver.so, cargo config, rand
 scheme, rand read)
 - ❌ rustc -vV, cargo build, binary execution — all blocked by the same ld_so bug

 ### The Remaining Blocker

 Redox's dynamic linker (ld_so in relibc) corrupts kernel scheme state during library loading. After ld_so finishes loading
 librustc_driver.so + libstdc++.so.6 + libc.so, the process's subsequent open("/scheme/rand") returns EBADF. Static binaries
 opening the same path work perfectly.

 ### Paths Forward (from plan.md Phase 2.5)

 1. Debug relibc's ld_so — trace what it does to fds/scheme state during loading
 2. Patch Rust std — use rand: URL instead of /scheme/rand (different kernel code path)
 3. Make rustc fully static — eliminate dynamic linking entirely (big binary but avoids ld_so)

What We've Built

 A self-hosting Rust development environment on Redox OS —
 32 tests prove you can write, compile, link, and run Rust
 programs entirely on-guest. This includes multi-file
 projects, CLI tools (minigrep), and manual build scripts.

 The One Remaining Gap

 Cargo build scripts with println! hang — the stdout pipe
 from the build-script process back to cargo doesn't work on
 Redox. This blocks building any crate with a build.rs
 (which is most real-world crates — serde, tokio, regex,
 etc.).

Current Status

 ### Tests Passing: 37/41

 - All 37 basic self-hosting tests pass (toolchain,
 compilation, cargo features, minigrep, real programs,
 multifile, build scripts, proc-macros, path deps, vendored
 deps)
 - 4 snix self-compile tests fail (snix-compile,
 snix-binary-exists, snix-binary-runs, snix-eval-works)

 ### Fixed This Session

 1. Heredoc indentation — 3 tests were failing because
 heredoc terminators were at wrong indentation level in Nix
 '' strings
 2. blake3 build script hang — CARGO_FEATURE_PURE not
 propagated to build scripts on Redox; patched blake3's
 build.rs to force pure Rust mode when targeting Redox
 3. Test reliability — Fixed false positive in snix-compile
 check (now verifies binary existence, not just exit code)

 ### Remaining Blocker: serde_derive proc-macro linking

 The snix self-compile fails because serde_derive (a
 proc-macro crate) can't be linked as a .so:
 - Primary error: ld.lld: error: unknown argument
 '-Wl,--version-script=...' — the CC wrapper's -Wl, prefix
 stripping doesn't work for ALL arguments passed to ld.lld
 - Secondary error: undefined symbol:
 std::rt::lang_start_internal — may be from a probe
 compilation
 - Root cause: The CC wrapper's argument processing needs to
 be more robust for shared library linking

 ### Next Steps

 1. Fix the CC wrapper's -Wl, stripping to handle all
 argument formats (comma-separated lists like
 -Wl,-z,relro,-z,now)
 2. Debug the lang_start_internal undefined symbol — may need
 to exclude certain object files from shared library links
 3. Once serde_derive links, the remaining ~45 crates should
 compile, completing the full snix self-compile on Redox

Here's the summary of what works:

 Yes, it can control the VM once logged in. The full tool chain:

 ┌───────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────┐
 │ Tool          │ What it does                                  │ Status │
 ├───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┤
 │ vm_boot       │ Start QEMU headless with QMP + serial         │ ✅     │
 ├───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┤
 │ vm_screenshot │ Capture VGA framebuffer as PNG                │ ✅     │
 ├───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┤
 │ vm_serial     │ Read boot logs, send commands (headless only) │ ✅     │
 ├───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┤
 │ vm_sendkey    │ Type text or send keys (Enter, Ctrl-C, etc.)  │ ✅     │
 ├───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┤
 │ vm_mouse      │ Click/move at pixel coordinates               │ ✅     │
 ├───────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┤
 │ vm_stop       │ Graceful shutdown via QMP                     │ ✅     │
 └───────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────┘

 What I proved today:
 - Mouse click focuses Orbital's password field (white border appears)
 - typeText("r") → one dot appears in password field
 - typeText("edox") → five dots total
 - sendKey("ret") → form submits (login succeeds, no red borders)
 - Login works with Argon2-hashed password in shadow file

 Two Redox-side bugs blocking full desktop usage:
 1. Shadow passwords need Argon2 hashing — the build module now writes the
 password field to shadow, but a proper hashPassword Nix helper (using
 libargon2 with a deterministic salt) is needed
